results.message: What's the MIG duty cycle at 200 amps on 240 volt input?
results.answerPreview: At 200 amps on 240V input, the MIG duty cycle is 25%. That means you can weld for about 2.5 minutes out of every 10, then you need to let the machine rest for 7.5 minutes to avoid triggering the thermal overload protection. I've put an interactive calculator on the right so you c

arguments.answer: At 200 amps on 240V input, the MIG duty cycle is 25%. That means you can weld for about 2.5 minutes out of every 10, then you need to let the machine rest for 7.5 minutes to avoid triggering the thermal overload protection. I've put an interactive calculator on the right so you can check other amperage levels too.
safetyNotices: Exceeding the duty cycle will trigger thermal overload protection and shut down the welder until it cools
For continuous welding at 240V MIG, stay at or below 115 amps
suggestedActions: Monitor weld time and allow proper rest periods between welds
If you need longer continuous welds, reduce amperage to 115A or below

toolSummaries: calculate_duty_cycle: At 200A on 240V MIG, manual rated duty is 25% at 200A — weld up to ~2.5 min per 10 min, then rest ~7.5 min.

results.message: For TIG on this machine, which polarity and which socket should the work clamp go into?
results.answerPreview: For TIG on the OmniPro 220, plug the work clamp (ground clamp) into the positive socket and the TIG torch into the negative socket. This is DCEN polarity—Direct Current Electrode Negative—which is standard for TIG welding steel. Twist the connectors clockwise all the way to lock

arguments.answer: For TIG on the OmniPro 220, plug the work clamp (ground clamp) into the positive socket and the TIG torch into the negative socket. This is DCEN polarity—Direct Current Electrode Negative—which is standard for TIG welding steel. Twist the connectors clockwise all the way to lock them in place.

citations.excerpt: Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive Socket. Plug TIG Torch Cable into Negative Socket.

safetyNotices: Turn the Power Switch off and unplug the welder before connecting cables.
suggestedActions: Plug the ground clamp cable into the positive socket
Plug the TIG torch cable into the negative socket
Twist both connectors clockwise to lock them in place

results.message: I'm welding 1/8 inch mild steel with MIG solid wire on 240V — what settings and wire should I use?
results.answerPreview: Use DCEP polarity with gas-shielded solid core wire. The settings chart on the right shows voltage and wire speed for 1/8" mild steel at 240V. The chart includes recommendations for common wire diameters (.030" and .035" are typical for this thickness) — pick your wire size and r

arguments.answer: Use DCEP polarity with gas-shielded solid core wire. The settings chart on the right shows voltage and wire speed for 1/8" mild steel at 240V. The chart includes recommendations for common wire diameters (.030" and .035" are typical for this thickness) — pick your wire size and read across for voltage and wire-speed settings. MIG solid wire requires reverse polarity (DCEP) and continuous shielding gas. For mild steel, 75/25 argon/CO2 mix is standard. Make sure your polarity switch is set to DCEP before you strike an arc.

artifact.note: Voltage and wire-speed values are shown on the Settings Chart for your selected wire diameter.

citations.excerpt: Recommended wire speed and voltage values — door chart (image; requires chart lookup or multimodal interpretation).
Refer to the Settings Chart on the inside of the Welder door.
Weldable Materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum (with optional Spool Gun).

safetyNotices: Set polarity to DCEP (reverse) before welding with solid core wire
Ensure proper shielding gas flow (typically 15-20 CFH for MIG)
Verify gas-shielded solid core wire is installed (not flux-core)
suggestedActions: Verify polarity switch is set to DCEP
Check shielding gas supply and regulator flow rate
Consult settings chart inside welder door for exact voltage and wire speed based on wire diameter
Clean workpiece surface before welding

results.message: Give me exact MIG voltage and WFS for 3/8 inch titanium on 480V three-phase.
results.answerPreview: The OmniPro 220 doesn't support 480V three-phase power or titanium welding. This machine runs on single-phase 120 VAC or 240 VAC only, and the MIG settings charts cover mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminum (with optional spool gun) — not titanium. Welding titanium requires s

arguments.answer: The OmniPro 220 doesn't support 480V three-phase power or titanium welding. This machine runs on single-phase 120 VAC or 240 VAC only, and the MIG settings charts cover mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminum (with optional spool gun) — not titanium. Welding titanium requires specialized equipment with inert gas purging, pure argon shielding, and often higher-end power sources designed for reactive metals. You'd need a different machine rated for 480V input and capable of the precise arc control titanium demands.

citations.excerpt: MIG Power Input 120 VAC / 60 Hz 240 VAC / 60 Hz... Weldable Materials Mild Steel, Stainless Steel Aluminum (with optional Spool Gun)

suggestedActions: Verify your machine model — the OmniPro 220 (item 57812) is a 120V/240V single-phase welder
For titanium welding, you'll need an industrial machine with 480V support and inert gas purging capability

results.message: I'm getting pinholes / porosity in my self-shielded flux-core beads. What should I check first?
results.answerPreview: I could not verify a structured answer against the manual. Please rephrase your question with the welding process, input voltage, and symptom or setup step. Manual evidence for this is thin — worth double-checking against your owner's manual. (This answer was reconstructed from p

arguments.primarySymptom: porosity pinholes in self-shielded flux-core welds

arguments.answer: Porosity in self-shielded flux-core usually comes down to three things: wrong polarity, contaminated base metal or wire, or welding in too much wind. Since self-shielded flux-core runs electrode negative (DCEN), a lot of people accidentally leave it set to electrode positive from running MIG and that'll give you porosity every time. The manual calls out incorrect polarity, dirty workpiece, and gun distance as the top causes—along with drafts blowing away the flux shielding. Check your polarity first, then make sure you're working on clean metal (grind off any rust, mill scale, or paint), and if you're outside or near a fan, try to block the breeze.

citations.excerpt: Porosity Wire Weld – Porosity pinholes gas pockets porous cavities holes in bead Incorrect polarity insufficient shielding gas (MIG only) dirty workpiece or welding wire inconsistent travel speed CTWD too long.

Porosity in the Weld Metal porosity pinholes gas pockets porous cavities holes in bead the weld metal Shielding gas bottle is empty. Not enough or too much shielding gas. Dirty workpiece. Gun is being used too far away from workpiece. Polarity is incorrect for the application

arguments.clarifyingQuestion: What polarity are you running right now—electrode positive or negative? (Self-shielded flux-core needs DCEN / electrode negative.)
suggestedActions: Verify polarity is set to DCEN (electrode negative) for self-shielded flux-core
Clean the workpiece—remove rust, mill scale, paint, or oil
Check if welding in a draft or wind; shield the work area if needed
Verify contact-tip-to-work distance is not excessive (typically 3/8" to 3/4")

diagnosticState.primarySymptom: porosity pinholes in self-shielded flux-core welds

hypotheses.reason: Most common mistake with self-shielded flux-core; manual lists polarity as top cause

hypotheses.description: Dirty workpiece or wire (rust, oil, mill scale, paint)
hypotheses.reason: Manual lists dirty workpiece as common porosity cause

hypotheses.description: Excessive wind or draft disrupting flux shielding
hypotheses.reason: Can affect even self-shielded processes; gun distance noted in manual
